Preview of all eight self-contained blocks on one page (in production each goes on its own page). Each renders its static tables and its interactive widget with no external files.

Container Load — container-load

A 20ft container holds about 19.0 tonnes of coconut shisha charcoal (≈ 950 × 20 kg cartons); a 40ft holds about 25.5 tonnes. Because the charcoal is dense, every standard container reaches its weight limit before it runs out of space — so payload is capped by tonnes, not cubic metres.

A 20ft container holds approximately 19.0 tonnes of coconut shisha charcoal, or about 950 × 20 kg master cartons (verified 2026-06).

A 40ft container holds approximately 25.5 tonnes of coconut shisha charcoal (verified 2026-06).

Because coconut shisha charcoal is dense, all standard containers reach their weight limit before their volume limit (verified 2026-06).

Net coconut-charcoal payload per container · verified 2026-06
ContainerNet charcoal20 kg cartonsLimited by
20ft Standard19.0 t950weight
40ft Standard25.5 t1,275weight
40ft High Cube26.0 t1,300weight

Full load matrix by carton and container

Cartons that fit × net tonnes, by carton and container · verified 2026-06
Master carton20ft Standard40ft Standard40ft High Cube
1kg (1 kg)19,000 / 19.0 t25,500 / 25.5 t26,000 / 26.0 t
10kg (10 kg)1,900 / 19.0 t2,550 / 25.5 t2,600 / 26.0 t
20kg (20 kg)950 / 19.0 t1,275 / 25.5 t1,300 / 26.0 t
24kg (24 kg)791 / 19.0 t1,062 / 25.5 t1,083 / 26.0 t

Cells show cartons that fit / resulting net tonnes. Packing assumes ≥30 cm headspace and ≤40°C packing per IMDG Amendment 42-24, Special Provision 978 (UN 1361, Class 4.2).

How this is calculated

Two limits are computed and the smaller wins. The weight limit is the container's maximum net payload divided by carton net weight; the volume limit is usable container volume (CBM × 88% packing efficiency) divided by carton volume. For coconut charcoal the weight limit is almost always the binding one, which is why load is quoted in tonnes.

Payloads shown use a practical net charcoal weight after the ≥30 cm headspace and ≤40°C packing required for UN 1361 (Class 4.2) under IMDG Special Provision 978. Carton dimensions here are illustrative — replace them with your real packaging for exact carton counts.

Frequently asked questions

How many tonnes of shisha charcoal fit in a 20ft container?

About 19.0 tonnes of coconut shisha charcoal in a 20ft container, and about 25.5 tonnes in a 40ft — the load is weight-limited, not volume-limited.

How many boxes of coconut charcoal fit in a container?

Roughly 950 × 20 kg cartons in a 20ft and 1,275 in a 40ft. Smaller cartons raise the box count but the total tonnage stays about the same.

Why is charcoal weight-limited and not volume-limited?

Coconut charcoal cubes are dense, so a container hits its maximum payload weight while space is still left over. That is the opposite of light, bulky cargo, which fills the volume first.

Estimate your own container load

Pick a carton size and container to see the load for your packaging.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Landed Cost — landed-cost

The landed cost of one 20ft container (18 t) of coconut shisha charcoal to the United States is about $32,417, or about $1.80/kg, at FOB terms. Coconut shell charcoal (HS 4402.20) enters the US duty-free under CBP ruling N306942, so duty is $0 on the customs value — verify any current reciprocal-tariff surcharge before you rely on it.

The landed cost of one 20ft container (18 t) of coconut shisha charcoal to the United States is approximately $32,417, or $1.80/kg, at FOB (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

Coconut shisha charcoal (HS 4402.20) enters the United States duty-free under CBP ruling N306942 (verified 2026-06; verify current reciprocal-tariff surcharges).

Landed cost per kilogram is the container's all-in door cost divided by net kilograms — for 18 t that is 18,000 kg (verified 2026-06).

Landed cost of one 18 t container by destination (FOB basis, illustrative freight) · verified 2026-06
DestinationGoods (FOB)Landed to door$/kgImport duty
United States$23,400$32,417$1.800% FOB
Germany$23,400$26,603$1.480% CIF
United Kingdom$23,400$27,678$1.540% CIF
Saudi Arabia$23,400$30,009$1.675% CIF
India$23,400$34,533$1.9230% CIF
Turkey$23,400$29,364$1.630% CIF
UAE$23,400$30,062$1.675% CIF
Canada$23,400$32,417$1.800% FOB
Australia$23,400$32,417$1.800% FOB

Ocean freight by container size

Ocean freight from Semarang (Indonesia) by container size · verified 2026-06
Destination20ft freight40ft freightOcean transit
United States$8,240$10,30052 days
Germany$2,450$3,85045 days
United Kingdom$3,520$6,35534 days
Saudi Arabia$4,450$6,25025 days
India$2,560$2,79526 days
Turkey$5,200$7,80042 days
UAE$4,500$4,80020 days
Canada$2,425$3,03538 days
Australia$1,500$2,52029 days

Freight and transit reflect current shipping data. Coconut charcoal is weight-limited, so a 20ft (≈ 19 t) is often the effective unit, while a 40ft (≈ 25.5 t) carries more charcoal per dollar of freight.

Supplier price by incoterm

Supplier invoice price by incoterm, per 18 t container · verified 2026-06
DestinationEXWFOBCIFDDP
United States$18,000$23,400$31,767$32,417
Germany$18,000$23,400$25,953$26,603
United Kingdom$18,000$23,400$27,028$27,678
Saudi Arabia$18,000$23,400$27,961$30,009
India$18,000$23,400$26,064$34,533
Turkey$18,000$23,400$28,714$29,364
UAE$18,000$23,400$28,012$30,062
Canada$18,000$23,400$31,767$32,417
Australia$18,000$23,400$31,767$32,417

The incoterm changes which line the supplier invoices, not the final cost to your door — the door total stays the same across incoterms (assuming no supplier margin on stages they arrange). See the Incoterms tool.

Cost breakdown for one lane

Cost breakdown — 18 t container, Semarang → Los Angeles (illustrative) · verified 2026-06
Cost componentAmount (USD)
Goods (FOB $1,300/t × 18 t)$23,400
Ocean freight$8,240
Marine insurance (0.4%)$127
Import duty (0% FOB)$0
Destination clearance + inland$650
Total landed to door$32,417

How this is calculated

Landed cost sums the goods value (FOB), ocean freight, marine insurance (0.4% of the CIF base), import duty, and destination clearance plus inland delivery. Duty is applied to the customs value — the FOB value in the US and Canada, the CIF value in the EU, UK and GCC — at each country's rate.

For Saudi Arabia and the UAE, a GCC common tariff of about 5% applies and SASO/SABER conformity (PCoC + SCoC) is mandatory. US duty is $0 for HS 4402.20 under CBP N306942, but the US–Indonesia tariff position is the most volatile input here — re-check it monthly. All freight and duty figures are illustrative, not quotes.

Frequently asked questions

How much does it cost to import a container of coconut charcoal to the US?

About $32,417 all-in to the door for one 18 t 20ft container at FOB (≈ $1.80/kg), on illustrative freight. Duty is $0 under HS 4402.20 / CBP N306942.

Is coconut charcoal duty-free into the United States?

Yes — coconut shell charcoal under HS 4402.20 is classified duty-free per CBP ruling N306942. A separate reciprocal-tariff surcharge on Indonesian origin can apply and changes often, so verify the current position before quoting.

Does the incoterm change the landed cost?

No. The incoterm decides who arranges and invoices each stage, but the total cost to your door is the same whether you buy EXW, FOB, CIF or DDP (assuming the supplier adds no margin on stages they cover).

Calculate your landed cost

Change the destination, incoterm, tonnage and rates to match your shipment.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Reseller Margin — profit-margin

At a landed cost of $1.80/kg and a 40% margin, coconut shisha charcoal resells at about $3.00/kg, yielding roughly $21,600 gross profit on an 18 t container. Note that a 40% markup is not a 40% margin — the same number gives a different price depending on the method.

At a landed cost of $1.80/kg and a 40% margin, coconut shisha charcoal resells at $3.00/kg, yielding about $21,600 gross profit per 18 t container (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

A 40% markup is not a 40% margin: on $1.80/kg cost, a 40% markup gives $2.52/kg but only a 28.6% margin (verified 2026-06).

To earn a true 50% margin on a $1.80/kg landed cost, coconut shisha charcoal must sell at $3.60/kg, giving about $32,400 gross profit per 18 t container (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

Margin vs markup on a $1.80/kg landed cost (the difference people miss) · verified 2026-06
Target %Markup → resale/kg…actual marginMargin → resale/kg…actual markup
20%$2.1616.7%$2.2525.0%
30%$2.3423.1%$2.5742.9%
40%$2.5228.6%$3.0066.7%
50%$2.7033.3%$3.60100.0%

Same percentage, two methods, different prices. “Margin” is profit as a share of the selling price; “markup” is profit as a share of cost.

Profit per container by margin

Reseller profit per container at a $1.80/kg landed cost · verified 2026-06
Target marginResale/kgProfit/kgGross profit / 18 t container
20% margin$2.25$0.45$8,100
30% margin$2.57$0.77$13,886
40% margin$3.00$1.20$21,600
50% margin$3.60$1.80$32,400

Margin or markup — which should you use?

Markup is calculated on your cost: a $1.80/kg cost with a 40% markup sells at $2.52/kg. Margin is calculated on your selling price: to earn a true 40% margin you sell at $3.00/kg. Distributors usually plan in margin because it maps directly to profitability; suppliers often quote markup. Mixing them silently erodes profit.

Figures are illustrative and exclude storage, breakage, marketing and financing costs. Enter your own landed cost — from the landed-cost tool — for numbers specific to your lane.

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between margin and markup?

Markup is profit as a percentage of cost; margin is profit as a percentage of the selling price. A 40% markup on $1.80/kg is only a 28.6% margin.

How much profit is there in a container of shisha charcoal?

At a $1.80/kg landed cost and a 40% margin, an 18 t container yields about $21,600 gross profit before operating costs.

Price your resale

Enter your landed cost and switch between margin and markup.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Transit Time — transit-time

Ocean transit for coconut shisha charcoal from Semarang, Indonesia to Los Angeles is about 52–52 days. Allowing for the mandatory 14-day weathering period, production and clearance, the full door-ready lead time is roughly 75–87 days. Red Sea and Hormuz diversions can add 10–20+ days on Europe and Gulf lanes.

Ocean transit from Semarang to Los Angeles for coconut shisha charcoal is approximately 52–52 days (verified 2026-06); Red Sea/Hormuz diversions can add 10–20+ days.

Charcoal requires a mandatory 14-day weathering period before packing under IMDG Special Provision 978, added on top of ocean transit (verified 2026-06).

Full door-ready lead time from Indonesia to Los Angeles, including weathering, production and clearance, is roughly 75–87 days (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

Ocean transit time, Semarang (Indonesia) → destination · verified 2026-06
Destination port (from Semarang)Ocean transit
Los Angeles / Long Beach52–52 days
New York30–45 days
Hamburg45–45 days
Felixstowe34–34 days
Jebel Ali20–20 days
Jeddah25–25 days
Nhava Sheva26–26 days
Mersin42–42 days

Red Sea diversions (Cape of Good Hope routing) and Hormuz disruption can add 10–20+ days to Europe and Gulf lanes. Ranges are illustrative and date-sensitive.

Full lead-time breakdown

Full lead time to door — Semarang → Los Angeles (no custom OEM print) · verified 2026-06
StageDuration
Weathering (mandatory, pre-packing)14 days
Production7–14 days
Ocean transit (Semarang → Los Angeles)52–52 days
Destination clearance2–7 days
Door-ready total75–87 days

Add 14–21 days for custom OEM-printed packaging. The 14-day weathering period before packing is required for UN 1361 under IMDG Special Provision 978.

How this is calculated

The headline is ocean transit only. The door-ready total adds the pre-shipment steps that actually gate a coconut-charcoal order: a mandatory 14-day weathering period before packing, production time, optional custom OEM print, and destination clearance. Weathering is not optional — it is a safety requirement for UN 1361 (Class 4.2) self-heating cargo under IMDG SP 978. Carrier fire-safety guidance from CINS (the Cargo Incident Notification System) identifies charcoal as a recurring cause of container fires, which is why the weathering period and ≥30 cm headspace are enforced.

Transit ranges are illustrative and highly route-sensitive. Red Sea diversions around the Cape of Good Hope and Strait of Hormuz disruptions materially extend Europe and Gulf lanes; treat any quoted range as date-stamped and confirm with your forwarder.

Frequently asked questions

How long does it take to ship coconut charcoal from Indonesia?

Ocean transit is about 52–52 days to Los Angeles and longer to Europe and the Gulf. Including the 14-day weathering period, production and clearance, plan 75–87 days door-ready to the US West Coast.

Why does charcoal need a 14-day weathering period?

Freshly produced charcoal can self-heat, so UN 1361 (Class 4.2) cargo must weather for 14 days before packing under IMDG Special Provision 978. It is a safety requirement that sits ahead of ocean transit in the timeline.

Estimate your transit and lead time

Pick your ports and whether custom OEM print is needed.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Volume Pricing — moq-pricing

Wholesale coconut shisha charcoal starts at a full-container MOQ of about 19 tonnes (one 20ft) at roughly $1,300/t. Scaling up cuts the unit price: a annual contract lands near $1,175/t, about 9.6% below the single-container rate.

The minimum order for wholesale coconut shisha charcoal is one full container — about 19 tonnes — at roughly $1,300/t (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

Ordering 2 × 40ft brings the unit price to about $1,240/t, saving roughly $3,060 versus a single 20ft container (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

At an annual-contract volume the unit price is about $1,175/t, around 9.6% below the single-container rate (verified 2026-06).

Coconut charcoal volume price breaks (illustrative) · verified 2026-06
Order size$/t$/kgVolumeOrder valueDiscountSaving vs 1×20ft
1 × 20ft$1,300$1.3019 t$24,700——
1 × 40ft$1,270$1.2726 t$32,3852.3%$765
2 × 40ft$1,240$1.2451 t$63,2404.6%$3,060
5 × 40ft$1,205$1.21128 t$153,6387.3%$12,113
Annual contract$1,175$1.18300 t$352,5009.6%$37,500

Discount and saving are measured against a single 20ft container. Illustrative price breaks, not a quote.

How volume pricing works

Coconut charcoal is sold on full-container (FCL) minimums, so the smallest order is one container of about 19 tonnes. Unit price falls as committed volume rises because fixed production, handling and freight-negotiation costs spread across more tonnes. The discount column measures each tier against a single 20ft container.

These are illustrative tiers to show the shape of volume pricing. Actual pricing depends on cube size and spec, destination, packaging (bulk vs OEM-printed) and contract terms — request a quote for firm numbers.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum order quantity for coconut shisha charcoal?

One full container — about 19 tonnes for a 20ft — is the typical MOQ, because the product ships on full-container-load terms.

How much cheaper is buying in larger volume?

Moving from a single 20ft to 2 × 40ft saves about $3,060 at these illustrative rates; an annual contract runs about 9.6% below the single-container unit price.

See your volume price break

Choose an order size to see the unit price and saving.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Incoterms — incoterms

Under FOB, the buyer of a coconut shisha charcoal container pays ocean freight, insurance, duty and destination costs; under CIF the seller covers freight and insurance to the destination port. Either way the total to your door is about $32,417 for an 18 t container — the incoterm changes who arranges each stage, not the final cost.

Under FOB, the buyer of a coconut shisha charcoal container pays ocean freight, insurance, duty and destination costs; under CIF the seller covers freight and insurance to the destination port (verified 2026-06).

For the same 18 t container, the supplier invoice is about $18,000 (EXW), $23,400 (FOB), $31,767 (CIF) and $32,417 (DDP), while the total to the buyer's door stays about $32,417 under all four (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

The incoterm decides who arranges and pays each shipping stage — not the final landed cost (verified 2026-06).

Who pays what — Incoterms 2020 (coconut charcoal container) · verified 2026-06
Cost stageEXWFOBCIFDDP
Loading at seller's worksBuyerSellerSellerSeller
Origin inland haulageBuyerSellerSellerSeller
Export customs clearanceBuyerSellerSellerSeller
Ocean freightBuyerBuyerSellerSeller
Marine insuranceBuyerBuyerSellerSeller
Destination port / THCBuyerBuyerBuyerSeller
Import duty & taxesBuyerBuyerBuyerSeller
Destination inland to doorBuyerBuyerBuyerSeller

Simplified summary of Incoterms® 2020 obligations for ocean shipment. “Buyer” means the buyer arranges and pays that stage.

What each incoterm costs

Cost split by incoterm — 18 t container to the US (illustrative) · verified 2026-06
IncotermSupplier invoicesBuyer then arrangesTotal to buyer's door
EXW$18,000$14,417$32,417
FOB$23,400$9,017$32,417
CIF$31,767$650$32,417
DDP$32,417$0$32,417

The total to the buyer's door is the same across incoterms — the incoterm only moves the line between “supplier invoices” and “buyer arranges” (assuming no supplier margin on covered stages).

EXW vs FOB vs CIF vs DDP

EXW (Ex Works) puts everything on the buyer from the seller's gate. FOB (Free On Board) means the seller handles origin costs and export clearance up to the ship; the buyer takes ocean freight, insurance, duty and destination. CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight) adds ocean freight and insurance to the destination port to the seller's side; the buyer still handles duty and destination delivery. DDP (Delivered Duty Paid) puts the whole chain, including import duty, on the seller.

Because the same stages must be paid by someone, the door total is invariant — what changes is who arranges each stage and where risk transfers. Obligations here summarise Incoterms® 2020; costs are illustrative, not a quote.

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between EXW, FOB, CIF and DDP?

They set who pays and arranges each shipping stage. EXW is buyer-does-everything; FOB adds origin handling and export to the seller; CIF adds ocean freight and insurance to the seller; DDP puts the entire chain including duty on the seller.

Is FOB or CIF cheaper for importing charcoal?

Neither is inherently cheaper — the total to your door is the same. CIF just bundles freight and insurance into the supplier's invoice; FOB lets you arrange them yourself, which can be cheaper if you have good freight rates.

Who pays import duty under CIF?

The buyer. Under CIF the seller covers freight and insurance only to the destination port; import duty, customs clearance and inland delivery remain the buyer's responsibility. Only DDP puts duty on the seller.

Compare incoterms for your shipment

Highlight an incoterm and adjust the shipment to see the split.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

ROI & Payback — roi-payback

A 20ft container of coconut shisha charcoal (about $32,417 landed) sold through in 60 days at a 35% margin returns roughly 53.8% per cycle and about 231.2% annualized, with payback in about 85 days. Faster sell-through multiplies the annualized return by turning the same capital more often.

A 20ft container of coconut shisha charcoal sold through in 60 days at a 35% margin returns about 53.8% per cycle and roughly 231.2% annualized (verified 2026-06; illustrative).

At a 35% margin the per-cycle ROI is fixed at about 53.8%; payback and annualized return depend on how fast inventory sells through (verified 2026-06).

Cash cycle is transit plus sell-through — about 85 days for a 60-day sell-through on a 25-day lane, giving roughly 4.3 inventory turns a year (verified 2026-06).

Return on a 18 t container at a 35% margin (25-day transit, illustrative) · verified 2026-06
Sell-throughROI / cycleTurns / yrPaybackAnnualized ROI
30 days53.8%6.655 days357.3%
60 days53.8%4.385 days231.2%
90 days53.8%3.2115 days170.9%
120 days53.8%2.5145 days135.5%

ROI per cycle is fixed by the margin; faster sell-through raises turns and annualized ROI. Capital = $32,417 landed; excludes storage, financing and breakage.

How this is calculated

Gross profit is revenue at full sell-through minus the landed capital; ROI per cycle is gross profit over capital. The cash cycle is ocean transit plus sell-through days; annual turns are 365 divided by the cycle, and annualized ROI is per-cycle ROI times turns. Payback at full sell-through equals the cash cycle.

The per-cycle return is set entirely by the margin — 53.8% at 35% here — so the lever that matters for annual performance is sell-through speed, which multiplies how many times the same capital works in a year. Figures are illustrative and exclude storage, financing, breakage and marketing.

Frequently asked questions

What is the ROI on importing a container of coconut charcoal?

At a 35% margin, an 18 t container returns about 53.8% gross per cycle. Sold through in 60 days on a 25-day lane that is roughly 231.2% annualized, before operating costs.

How fast does a charcoal container pay back?

Payback at full sell-through equals the cash cycle — transit plus sell-through days. For a 60-day sell-through on a 25-day lane, that is about 85 days.

Model your return and payback

Enter your capital, margin, sell-through and transit.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.

Spec Comparison — spec-comparison

Premium coconut shisha charcoal typically has ash content below 2.5%, fixed carbon above 80%, moisture under 5%, and a burn time of 75+ minutes. Ash above about 4% or fixed carbon below about 72% generally signals standard rather than premium grade.

Premium coconut shisha charcoal typically has ash content below 2.5%, fixed carbon above 80%, moisture under 5%, and a burn time of 75+ minutes (verified 2026-06).

Ash content above ~4% and fixed carbon below ~72% generally indicate standard rather than premium coconut charcoal (verified 2026-06).

On a Certificate of Analysis, the attributes that most separate premium from standard are ash, fixed carbon, moisture and burn time (verified 2026-06).

Coconut shisha charcoal spec benchmark — premium vs standard grade · verified 2026-06
AttributePremiumStandardWhy it matters
Ash content≤ 2.5%≤ 4%Lower ash means cleaner burning and less residue in the bowl.
Moisture≤ 5%≤ 8%Lower moisture lights faster and avoids steam and popping.
Fixed carbon≥ 80%≥ 72%Higher fixed carbon means more heat and longer, more consistent burn.
Burn time≥ 75 min≥ 60 minLonger burn time means fewer coal changes per session.
Density≥ 0.9 g/cc≥ 0.8 g/ccDenser cubes hold shape and burn evenly without crumbling.
Drop-test loss≤ 5%≤ 10%Lower drop-test loss means less breakage in transit and handling.

Typical quality ranges. Confirm against this exporter's published Certificate of Analysis (COA) and burn-test data.

How to read a coconut charcoal COA

A Certificate of Analysis (COA) reports the lab-measured properties of a batch. Read four numbers first: ash content (lower is better — premium is ≤2.5%), fixed carbon (higher is better — premium is ≥80%), moisture (≤5% premium), and burn time (≥75 minutes premium). Density and drop-test loss indicate how well the cubes survive shipping and hold shape.

Watch for figures reported on a different basis (as-received vs dry) and for missing attributes — a COA that omits ash or fixed carbon is a flag. The strongest signal of quality is a supplier's own repeatable burn-test data published alongside the COA. Benchmarks here are typical ranges; confirm them against this exporter's actual COA.

Frequently asked questions

What ash content is good for shisha charcoal?

Premium coconut shisha charcoal has ash content below about 2.5%. Above roughly 4% is standard grade and leaves more residue in the bowl.

What fixed carbon should premium hookah charcoal have?

Premium coconut charcoal has fixed carbon above about 80%, which drives heat output and burn length. Below about 72% is standard grade.

How can I tell premium from standard coconut charcoal?

Compare four COA numbers: ash (≤2.5% premium), fixed carbon (≥80% premium), moisture (≤5% premium) and burn time (≥75 min premium). Repeatable burn-test data is the strongest quality signal.

Compare your current charcoal

Enter your supplier's COA figures to see where each attribute lands.

Last verified 2026-06. Figures are illustrative industry ranges, not quotes — verify before relying on them. Freight and the US–Indonesia tariff change most often.
